cards.q: Why are fungal skin infections so common in our environment, and what do they affect?
cards.lecturer: Spores of fungi occur in the hot and humid or arid tropical environment. Poor personal and community hygiene, closeness among large families and institutions such as schools, camps and barracks favour the spread of fungal infections among children. Most of these fungal infections affect the skin, hair, nails and mucosa.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: What does a fungus need to grow?

**Warmth** and **moisture**.

That is essentially the whole story.

---

>> A hot, humid tropical climate is not simply *where* these infections happen — it is **why** they happen.

The environment is a permanent incubator.

---

## Step 2: But the lecturer also says "arid"

Why would a **dry** environment favour fungi?

---

Because fungal **spores** are different from the growing fungus.

>> Spores are tough, dormant survival forms. Dryness does not kill them — it lets them **persist in dust and soil** and be carried on the wind.

So: **arid = spores survive and spread. Humid = spores germinate and grow.**

Both environments contribute, at different stages.

---

## Step 3: Why does crowding matter so much?

Because these fungi spread by **contact** — skin to skin, and via shared objects.

---

The lecturer names three settings:

- **Large families living closely**
- **Schools**
- **Camps and barracks**

---

**What do all three have in common?**

>> Many people, in close contact, **sharing** combs, caps, towels, bedding, clothing and floors.

That is exactly how tinea capitis travels through a classroom.

---

## Step 4: Why hygiene — and note the word "community"

>> "Poor **personal and community** hygiene."

**Personal** — infrequent washing, damp clothing, unwashed feet, occlusive shoes.

**Community** — shared water, shared washing facilities, no way to launder bedding separately.

---

>> You cannot cure a child of tinea capitis and send him back to share the same comb with four brothers. **The community is part of the treatment.**

---

## Step 5: Why skin, hair, nails and mucosa specifically?

This is the most useful part of the card.

---

**Superficial fungi feed on KERATIN.**

Keratin is the tough structural protein found in:

- **Skin** — the stratum corneum
- **Hair**
- **Nails**

>> These fungi are literally **eating the dead outer layer** of you.

---

**And mucosa?**

Mucosa has little keratin — which is why the mucosal infection is **not** a dermatophyte but **Candida**, a yeast that behaves differently.

---

>> So the four sites are not a random list. **Three are keratin (dermatophytes). One is mucosa (Candida).**

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Hot and humid grows them. Crowding spreads them. Keratin feeds them.**

And this explains why treatment always has two halves: **kill the fungus**, and **change the conditions** — dry the feet, stop sharing combs, improve hygiene.
cards.tie:
Hold on to the keratin idea.

It explains why tinea attacks skin, hair and nails; why tinea capitis needs **oral** treatment (the fungus is inside the hair shaft); and why pityriasis versicolor lives in the **stratum corneum**.

cards.q: How are fungal skin infections classified, and what is the practical difference between the groups?
cards.lecturer: Superficial: candidiasis, tinea capitis, tinea corporis, tinea pedis, pityriasis versicolor. Deep: mycetoma (Madura foot), blastomycosis, coccidiomycosis, histoplasmosis, sporotrichosis.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Five and five — learn the shape first

| **SUPERFICIAL** | **DEEP** |
|---|---|
| Candidiasis | Mycetoma (Madura foot) |
| Tinea capitis | Blastomycosis |
| Tinea corporis | Coccidiomycosis |
| Tinea pedis | Histoplasmosis |
| Pityriasis versicolor | Sporotrichosis |

---

## Step 2: The difference is not just depth. It is a completely different disease pattern.

This is the point that makes the classification worth having.

---

### SUPERFICIAL infections

**Where?** Skin, hair, nails, mucosa — the **outer, keratinised** layers

**How acquired?** **Direct contact** — person, surface, pet

**How diagnosed?** **KOH examination of scrapings**

**How treated?** **Topical** antifungals, or **oral** if extensive

**Is the child systemically ill?** **No** — it is uncomfortable and unsightly, not dangerous

---

### DEEP infections

**Where?** Lungs, bone, subcutaneous tissue, viscera

**How acquired?** Mostly **INHALATION of spores** — with one exception

**Where does it start?** **The LUNGS** — then spreads by **blood**

**How treated?** **IV amphotericin B**, or oral azoles

**Is the child systemically ill?** **Yes** — these are serious systemic diseases

---

## Step 3: The pattern that unlocks three of the five deep mycoses

Look carefully at histoplasmosis, blastomycosis and coccidiomycosis.

>> **All three:** acquired by **inhalation** → **primary infection in the LUNGS** → **haematogenous spread** → skin lesions.

**Identical pattern. Three times.**

---

>> Learn the pattern once, then just learn what makes each one different:

- **Histoplasmosis** → spreads to liver, bone marrow, spleen, lymph nodes, subcutaneous tissue. Found in **bat and poultry droppings**.
- **Blastomycosis** → spreads to skin, **bone and genital tract**. **Budding cells** on KOH.
- **Coccidiomycosis** → spreads to skin, subcutaneous tissue and **bones**. **Panniculitis, abscesses, draining sinuses**.

---

## Step 4: The two that break the pattern

**SPOROTRICHOSIS** — **not inhaled**. It is **implanted** by a **thorn prick**. *Sporothrix schenckii* grows on thorny plants.

**MYCETOMA** — also **implanted**, by a **splinter or thorn**, into the **foot**. And uniquely, it can be caused by **either fungi or bacteria**.

---

>> So among the deep mycoses: **three are inhaled, two are implanted.** That single sentence organises the whole group.

---

## Step 5: Why does the classification change what you do?

Because it changes where you look and what you give.

---

**Superficial** → scrape the skin, use KOH, give a cream.

**Deep** → think about the **lungs**, take cultures from **sputum, bone marrow, biopsy**, and give **systemic** antifungals.

---

>> A patient with skin nodules and a chronic cough does not have a skin disease. He has a **lung disease with skin manifestations**.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Superficial = keratin, contact, KOH, creams, not dangerous.**

>> **Deep = lungs (mostly), blood spread, biopsy and culture, amphotericin B, serious.**
cards.tie:
This classification is your first question when you meet any fungal lesion:

**Is this an outside-in problem, or an inside-out problem?**

The answer determines whether you scrape it or investigate the chest.

cards.q: Describe candidiasis and oral thrush, including the sign that confirms the diagnosis.
cards.lecturer: Candidiasis is an infection caused by Candida albicans, C. tropicalis, C. stellatoidea, C. krusei, or other yeasts of the genus Candida. The infections usually occur on the skin, mucous membranes, nail folds and nails, gastrointestinal tract and genital tract; in systemic candida infections the viscera may be involved. The skin infection may present as dusky red macerated or erosive lesions in the skin folds of the neck, flanks, large flexures and limbs. Fissuring and bleeding may follow the scratching of the affected areas with occurrence of secondary bacterial infection. Oral candidiasis is characterized by curdy or whitish-grey, friable, cheesy pseudomembranous patches or plaques on markedly reddened mucosa. The plaques invade the mucosa superficially and may be found on the lips, buccal mucosa, tongue and palate extending to oesophagus/pharynx. Removal of plaques from these surfaces may cause mild punctate areas of bleeding, which helps to confirm the diagnosis. Predisposing factors include diabetes mellitus, retroviral disease, prolonged antibiotic and corticosteroid use, and leukaemia.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Candida is a YEAST, not a dermatophyte

This matters, and it explains everything that follows.

---

**Dermatophytes** (the tineas) eat **keratin** — so they attack skin, hair and nails.

**Candida** does not need keratin.

>> That is why *Candida* can infect **mucosa** — mouth, gut, genital tract — where the tineas cannot go.

---

## Step 2: Where does Candida live normally?

Here is the crucial point: ***Candida* is already living in you right now.**

In the mouth, the gut, the vagina — as a harmless part of the normal flora.

---

>> So candidiasis is **usually not caught from outside**. It is an **overgrowth of what is already there**.

That reframes the whole disease.

---

## Step 3: Now the predisposing factors make perfect sense

Ask of each one: *how does this let Candida overgrow?*

---

**Prolonged antibiotic use**

>> Antibiotics kill **bacteria** — including the friendly bacteria keeping *Candida* in check.

Remove the competition, and the yeast takes over. This is the same idea as **bacterial interference** from the bacterial skin lecture.

---

**Prolonged corticosteroid use**

Steroids **suppress immunity**, so the normal control is lost.

---

**Diabetes mellitus**

Two mechanisms: **high sugar feeds the yeast**, and diabetes **impairs immunity**.

---

**Retroviral disease (HIV)**

Destroys cell-mediated immunity — the exact arm that controls fungi.

>> This is why **oral thrush in an older child or adult with no other explanation should prompt an HIV test.**

---

**Leukaemia**

Malignancy plus chemotherapy = profound immunosuppression.

---

>> **Common thread: kill the competition, or weaken the host.**

---

## Step 4: What does skin candidiasis look like, and why THERE?

**Dusky red, macerated or erosive lesions** in the skin folds of the **neck, flanks, large flexures and limbs**.

---

**Why the folds?**

>> Skin folds are **warm, moist and occluded** — trapped against each other, unable to dry.

That is a yeast's ideal habitat.

**Macerated** means softened and broken down by moisture.

---

**Then the complication:**

Scratching → **fissuring and bleeding** → **secondary bacterial infection**.

>> The fungus opens the door, and bacteria walk in. Exactly like athlete's foot leading to cellulitis.

---

## Step 5: Oral thrush — get the description exact

>> **Curdy or whitish-grey, friable, cheesy pseudomembranous patches or plaques on markedly reddened mucosa.**

Every word earns its place:

- **Curdy / cheesy** — looks like curdled milk
- **Friable** — crumbles easily
- **Pseudomembranous** — a false membrane sitting on the surface, not part of it
- **On markedly reddened mucosa** — the background is inflamed

---

**Sites:** lips, buccal mucosa, tongue, palate — and it can extend to **oesophagus and pharynx**.

>> Oesophageal extension causes painful swallowing, and is a marker of significant immunosuppression.

---

## Step 6: The confirming sign — and the reasoning behind it

>> **Removal of the plaques causes mild punctate areas of bleeding.**

Why does that confirm the diagnosis?

---

**Step 1:** The plaques **invade the mucosa superficially** — they are not just lying on top.

**Step 2:** So they are **anchored** into the surface layer.

**Step 3:** Scraping them off tears those tiny anchor points.

**Step 4:** Each torn point bleeds — hence **punctate** (pinpoint) bleeding.

---

>> **Compare with milk curds in a baby's mouth:** those wipe away cleanly, leaving normal pink mucosa and **no bleeding**.

That is the bedside test, and it needs nothing but a spatula.

---

## Step 7: Treatment

**Clotrimazole · nystatin · amphotericin B · miconazole gel** — plus **proper oral hygiene**.

>> **Nystatin** and **miconazole gel** are the workhorses in a baby — applied directly, poorly absorbed, so they act locally with minimal systemic effect.

And hygiene matters: sterilise bottles and teats, or you re-infect the baby at every feed.

---

## Putting it all together

>> ***Candida* is already there.** Disease happens when you **kill its competitors** or **weaken the host**.

>> **The plaque that bleeds when scraped is thrush. The one that wipes off cleanly is milk.**
cards.tie:
Always ask **why** this patient has thrush.

In a neonate it is usually normal.

In an older child it is a **question**, not just a diagnosis — antibiotics? steroids? diabetes? HIV? leukaemia?

cards.q: Describe tinea capitis, including kerion and favus.
cards.lecturer: Tinea capitis is a dermatophyte infection of the scalp most often caused by Trichophyton tonsurans, occasionally by Microsporum canis, and much less commonly by other Microsporum and Trichophyton spp. Lesions occur as erythematous and scaly circular plaques (ringworm) within which the infected hairs become brittle and broken with different lengths. Multiple affected sites produce a moth-eaten appearance of hair. Scaling and inflammation of scalp are variable. Numerous confluent patches of alopecia develop, and patients may complain of severe pruritus. Sometimes the host's cellular immune response may lead to the development of an inflammatory and infected boggy mass covered in crusts, pus-filled bumps and localized hair loss, referred to as a kerion. Favus is a severe chronic form typically caused by Trichophyton schoenleinii, characterized by thick yellowish cup-shaped yellow crusts called scutula that develop around hair follicles and often accompanied by a distinctive mousy odour; also termed honey-combed scalp lesions. If left untreated it destroys the hair follicles, causes matting of the hair and permanent scarring hair loss.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Why does the hair break rather than fall out?

This is the key to recognising tinea capitis.

---

**Step 1:** The dermatophyte eats **keratin**.

**Step 2:** Hair is **made of keratin**.

**Step 3:** The fungus grows **into the hair shaft** and consumes it from within.

**Step 4:** The shaft becomes **brittle** and snaps.

---

>> So the hairs are **broken at different lengths**, not shed from the root.

And that is why multiple patches give the **"moth-eaten" appearance** — like cloth chewed unevenly by insects.

---

>> **This also explains why topical treatment fails.** The fungus is *inside* the hair shaft, where a cream cannot reach. You need an **oral** drug carried there in the blood.

---

## Step 2: The basic lesion

**Erythematous and scaly circular plaques** — hence the name **ringworm**.

Plus: variable scaling and inflammation, **confluent patches of alopecia**, and **severe pruritus**.

---

>> Note: no worm is involved. "Ringworm" describes the **ring shape**, not a parasite. Patients often need this explained.

---

## Step 3: KERION — and the surprise in the mechanism

Read the lecturer's wording very carefully:

>> "The **host's cellular immune response** to tinea capitis may lead to the development of an inflammatory and infected boggy mass..."

---

**The kerion is caused by the child's OWN immune system.**

Not by the fungus being more aggressive.

---

**What happens:**

- A vigorous cellular immune response attacks the fungus in the scalp
- The inflammation is intense
- The result is a **boggy, swollen mass** with **crusts, pus-filled bumps and hair loss**

---

>> **Why this matters practically:** a kerion looks like a bacterial abscess. It is boggy, crusted and pustular.

**The temptation is to incise and drain it, or to give antibiotics.**

>> Both are wrong. It is a **fungal** problem with an **immune** reaction — and it needs an **antifungal**. Incising it risks scarring.

---

## Step 4: FAVUS — the severe chronic form

**Organism:** ***Trichophyton schoenleinii*** — a different organism from ordinary tinea capitis.

---

**The signature lesion — scutula:**

>> Thick, yellowish, **cup-shaped** crusts that develop **around hair follicles**.

*Scutulum* means "little shield" — they look like small yellow cups or saucers sitting on the scalp.

---

**And a genuinely unusual sign:**

>> A **distinctive mousy odour.**

Smell is rarely diagnostic in medicine. Here it is. Examiners love it.

---

**Also described as "honey-combed" scalp lesions** — because the merging cup-shaped crusts look like honeycomb.

---

## Step 5: Why is favus so serious?

>> Untreated, it **destroys the hair follicles**, causes **matting of the hair**, and results in **permanent scarring alopecia**.

---

**Understand the word "scarring":**

- **Non-scarring alopecia** — the follicle survives, so hair **regrows** after treatment. This is ordinary tinea capitis.
- **Scarring alopecia** — the follicle is **destroyed** and replaced by scar tissue. **Hair never regrows.**

---

>> This is why favus must be treated early. Every week of delay costs follicles that can never be replaced.

---

## Step 6: Diagnosis and treatment

**Diagnosis:** **scraping and KOH examination**.

>> KOH (potassium hydroxide) dissolves the keratin of the skin and hair, but **not** the fungal cell wall — so the fungal hyphae stand out clearly under the microscope.

Simple, cheap, and available anywhere.

---

**Treatment:**

- **ORAL griseofulvin, itraconazole or terbinafine**
- **Topical** antifungal cream or shampoo

---

>> The oral drug is **essential** — it reaches the fungus inside the hair shaft.

>> The shampoo is **adjunctive** — it reduces spores on the scalp surface and so **reduces spread to other children**.

**Treating the head means treating the classroom too.**

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Broken hairs at different lengths = tinea capitis.**

>> **Boggy inflamed mass = kerion — the immune response, not a bacterial abscess.**

>> **Yellow cup-shaped scutula + mousy smell = favus — and it scars permanently.**

>> **Always treat orally.**
cards.tie:
Tinea capitis is the clearest demonstration of the keratin principle.

The fungus lives **inside** the hair — which dictates the broken-hair sign, the failure of creams, and the need for oral therapy.

cards.q: Describe tinea corporis and tinea pedis, and how each is diagnosed and treated.
cards.lecturer: Tinea corporis is fungal infection of the glabrous skin, excluding the palms, soles and groin. The most common aetiologic organisms are Trichophyton rubrum and Trichophyton mentagrophytes; infections with Microsporum canis are also frequent. It characteristically appears as a dry, mildly erythematous, elevated, scaly papule or plaque that spreads centrifugally and clears centrally to form the characteristic annular lesion; the active edge shows vesicles and pustules. It is usually acquired from direct contact from infected persons, surfaces and pets. Other forms include tinea faciale, tinea axillaris, tinea cruris, tinea manuum and tinea unguium. Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) is a fungal infection of the toe webs and soles of the feet, caused by Trichophyton rubrum, Trichophyton mentagrophytes and Epidermophyton floccosum. The subdigital crevices are fissured, with maceration and peeling of the surrounding skin; less commonly a chronic diffuse hyperkeratosis of the sole occurs with only mild erythema; occasionally the papules progress to vesicles and bullae that may become pustular. Severe tenderness, itching and a persistent foul odour are characteristic.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: What does "glabrous" mean, and why the exclusions?

**Glabrous** = smooth and hairless.

---

**Tinea corporis excludes palms, soles and groin.**

**Why?** Because those sites have their **own names**:

- Palms → **tinea manuum**
- Soles → **tinea pedis**
- Groin → **tinea cruris**

>> It is not that the fungus cannot go there. It is that the naming system is by **site**.

---

## Step 2: The naming system — learn it once and it covers everything

>> **"Tinea" + the Latin name of the body part.**

| Name | Site |
|---|---|
| Tinea **capitis** | Head |
| Tinea **corporis** | Body |
| Tinea **faciale** | Face |
| Tinea **axillaris** | Axilla |
| Tinea **cruris** | Groin |
| Tinea **manuum** | Hands |
| Tinea **pedis** | Feet |
| Tinea **unguium** | Nails |

>> Same organisms. Same disease. **The name just tells you where.**

---

## Step 3: Why is tinea corporis RING-shaped? (The best part of this topic.)

This is not decoration — it tells you where the living fungus is.

---

**Step 1:** The fungus lands on one spot and starts growing.

**Step 2:** It grows **outwards in all directions** — the lecturer's word is **centrifugally** (away from the centre).

**Step 3:** At the **centre**, the fungus has already consumed the keratin — and the immune system has had time to clear it. So the centre **heals**.

**Step 4:** At the **edge**, the fungus is meeting fresh, untouched skin. **That is where it is actively growing.**

---

>> The result is a **ring**: clear in the middle, active at the rim. An **annular lesion**.

---

**And this is why the lecturer specifies the ACTIVE EDGE shows vesicles and pustules:**

- The edge = active infection + inflammation
- The centre = already cleared

---

>> **Practical consequence:** when you take a scraping for KOH, **scrape the EDGE, not the centre.** Scrape the middle and you may find nothing at all.

That single fact turns a memorised description into a usable skill.

---

## Step 4: Where is it caught from?

**Infected persons · surfaces · PETS.**

---

>> ***Microsporum canis*** — the clue is in the name. ***Canis*** **= dog.**

Also cats.

---

>> **So ask about pets.** And if there is an infected pet, treat the pet — or the child will be re-infected.

---

## Step 5: Tinea pedis — why the toe webs?

Go back to what a fungus needs: **warmth and moisture**.

---

**The space between the toes is:**

- **Warm** — enclosed in a shoe
- **Moist** — sweat with nowhere to evaporate
- **Occluded** — surfaces pressed together
- **Dark**

>> It is the single most fungus-friendly location on the body.

Hence the name **athlete's foot** — sport means sweat plus enclosed shoes.

---

## Step 6: The three clinical patterns

**Pattern 1 (commonest):** subdigital crevices **fissured**, with **maceration and peeling** of surrounding skin

**Pattern 2 (less common):** chronic diffuse **hyperkeratosis of the sole** with only mild erythema — thickened, dry, scaly sole

**Pattern 3 (occasional):** papules progressing to **vesicles and bullae** that may become **pustular**

---

**And the characteristic symptoms:**

>> **Severe tenderness, itching, and a persistent foul odour.**

The smell comes from fungal breakdown of keratin plus secondary bacterial overgrowth in the moist macerated skin.

---

## Step 7: The connection you must not miss

>> Those **fissures** between the toes are a **break in the skin barrier**.

And from the bacterial skin lecture: **athlete's foot is a listed predisposing factor for CELLULITIS.**

---

>> A trivial itchy foot becomes the doorway for a limb-threatening bacterial infection.

**Treating the fungus prevents the bacteria.** The two lectures are talking to each other.

---

## Step 8: Diagnosis — the same tool for both

**Skin scraping + KOH examination**, showing **fungal mycelia**.

**Culture** identifies the exact species.

---

>> **KOH dissolves human keratin but not the fungal cell wall**, so the hyphae stand out under the microscope. Cheap, quick, available anywhere.

---

## Step 9: Treatment

**Tinea corporis**

- **Topical** — imidazoles, terbinafine, naftifine
- **Severe** → oral griseofulvin, itraconazole

>> Topical works here because the fungus is in **skin**, not inside hair shafts. Contrast with tinea capitis, which always needs oral.

---

**Tinea pedis**

- **Avoid occlusive footwear**
- **Dry carefully between the toes after bathing**
- Topical **zinc undecylenate powder**, **imidazole cream**, oral **griseofulvin**

---

>> Notice that the **first two are not drugs at all.** They remove the moisture the fungus needs.

**Powder** is used deliberately rather than cream — it **absorbs moisture** as well as delivering the drug.

>> Cure the fungus and put him back in wet closed shoes, and he will be back.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **The ring tells you where the fungus is living — scrape the edge.**

>> **The toe web is the ideal fungal habitat — so drying it is treatment, not advice.**
cards.tie:
Two ideas to carry forward:

- **Scrape the active edge**, not the healed centre
- **Tinea pedis is the doorway to cellulitis** — this links directly to the bacterial skin infections lecture

cards.q: Describe pityriasis versicolor — its organism, predisposing factors, appearance and treatment.
cards.lecturer: Pityriasis versicolor is a common chronic fungal infection of the stratum corneum, caused by the dimorphic yeast Malassezia furfur, found in areas of skin that are rich in sebum production. The disease is most prevalent in adolescents and young adults. Predisposing factors include a warm humid environment, excessive sweating, occlusion, high plasma cortisol levels, immunosuppression, malnourishment, and genetically determined susceptibility. The lesions may be either hypopigmented or hyperpigmented macules covered with a fine scale, that often merge to form confluent patches. It occurs most commonly on the neck, upper chest, back and upper arms. Diagnosis: Wood lamp, KOH preparation of scrapings, skin biopsy. Treatment: selenium sulfide suspension applied overnight; imidazole or terbinafine cream twice daily for 2–4 weeks; oral therapy with ketoconazole, fluconazole or itraconazole.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: What does "versicolor" mean?

**Versi** = varying. **Color** = colour.

>> The name tells you the diagnosis: the lesions **vary in colour** — some patches lighter than surrounding skin, some darker, sometimes both on the same person.

---

**Hypopigmented** = lighter than normal skin

**Hyperpigmented** = darker than normal skin

---

>> Very few skin conditions can go **both ways**. When you see that, think pityriasis versicolor.

---

## Step 2: Where exactly does it live?

>> The **stratum corneum** — the outermost, dead, keratinised layer of the epidermis.

**Extremely superficial.** It does not even reach living tissue.

---

**Two consequences follow:**

- It is **not dangerous** and causes no systemic illness
- **Topical treatment works well**, because the drug reaches the whole depth of the infection

---

## Step 3: Why does it grow where it does?

**_Malassezia furfur_ is found in areas of skin rich in SEBUM production.**

---

>> This yeast is **lipophilic** — it feeds on the fats in sebum.

So it grows where the sebaceous glands are busiest.

---

**Now look at the sites:**

**Neck · upper chest · back · upper arms**

>> Exactly the sebum-rich areas of the body. The distribution is not random — **it is a map of sebaceous gland density.**

---

## Step 4: Why adolescents and young adults?

Because of what happens at puberty.

---

**Step 1:** Androgens surge at puberty.

**Step 2:** Androgens **stimulate sebaceous glands**.

**Step 3:** Sebum production rises sharply.

**Step 4:** More food for a lipophilic yeast.

---

>> This is the same reason acne appears at the same age, in the same places. **Follow the sebum.**

---

## Step 5: The predisposing factors — group them

---

**Group A — More moisture and warmth**

- Warm humid environment
- Excessive sweating
- **Occlusion** (tight clothing, trapped skin)

---

**Group B — Weakened host defence**

- **High plasma cortisol** — cortisol suppresses immunity and increases sebum
- **Immunosuppression**
- **Malnourishment**

---

**Group C — The host himself**

- **Genetically determined susceptibility**

>> This explains why one person in a family gets it repeatedly and others never do, in the same house and the same climate.

---

## Step 6: Why does it change the skin colour at all?

This is the part worth understanding rather than accepting.

---

**The hypopigmented patches:**

>> *Malassezia* produces **dicarboxylic acids** which **inhibit tyrosinase** — the key enzyme in melanin production.

Less melanin made → the patch is **lighter** than surrounding skin.

---

>> **A practical point patients need to hear:** after successful treatment, the fungus is gone but the **pale patches persist for months**, until melanin production recovers.

Warn them, or they will think the treatment failed and keep applying creams.

---

## Step 7: The Wood lamp

A **Wood lamp** emits **ultraviolet light**.

>> Under it, pityriasis versicolor **fluoresces** — typically a golden-yellow colour.

---

**Why is this useful?**

- It shows the **full extent** of the disease, including patches invisible in ordinary light
- It is instant, painless and needs no laboratory

---

**The other two:**

- **KOH preparation of scrapings** — shows the classic mixture of short hyphae and round spores
- **Skin biopsy** — rarely needed

---

## Step 8: Treatment — and why selenium sulfide is first

---

**Selenium sulfide suspension, applied overnight**

>> Why overnight? Because it needs **prolonged contact time** to work, and it is applied over a **large surface area** — chest, back, neck, arms.

It is cheap and covers wide areas, which creams do poorly.

---

**Imidazole or terbinafine cream, twice daily for 2–4 weeks**

>> Note the **duration**. This is a **chronic** infection — a few days of cream will not clear it.

---

**Oral ketoconazole, fluconazole or itraconazole**

For extensive or resistant disease.

---

## Step 9: Why does it come back so often?

Because ***Malassezia* is part of normal skin flora.**

---

>> You are not eradicating an invader. You are **reducing an overgrowth of a resident**.

And the predisposing factors — heat, humidity, sweating, sebum, genetics — are all still there.

**Recurrence is expected. Tell the patient in advance.**

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Lipophilic yeast + sebum-rich skin + adolescence + heat and sweat = versicolor patches on the neck, chest, back and arms.**

>> **Wood lamp confirms it. Selenium sulfide overnight treats it. The pale patches take months to repigment.**
cards.tie:
This is the most superficial infection in the lecture — confined to dead keratin in the stratum corneum.

Which is exactly why it is harmless, why creams work, and why it recurs so readily.

cards.q: Compare the deep mycoses — histoplasmosis, blastomycosis, coccidiomycosis, sporotrichosis and mycetoma.
cards.lecturer: Histoplasmosis is a chronic infection caused by Histoplasma capsulatum and H. duboisii, dimorphic fungi acquired by inhalation of fungal spores found in soil and droppings of animals such as bats and poultry; the primary infection is in the lungs with haematogenous spread to liver, bone marrow, spleen, lymph nodes and subcutaneous tissue. H. duboisii (African histoplasmosis) presents as subcutaneous nodules which tend to umbilicate and suppurate or ulcerate. Blastomycosis is caused by Blastomyces dermatitidis, acquired by inhalation, with primary infection in the lungs and haematogenous spread to skin, bone and genital tract. Coccidiomycosis is caused by Coccidioides immitis, acquired by inhalation, with primary lung infection and spread to skin, subcutaneous tissue and bones; skin lesions consist of panniculitis, abscesses and draining sinuses. Sporotrichosis follows accidental implantation into the skin of Sporothrix schenckii, which grows on thorny plants. Mycetoma is a chronic infection of the skin, subcutaneous tissue and bones of the feet, introduced through minor injuries like a splinter or thorn prick; eumycetoma when caused by fungi and actinomycetoma when caused by filamentous bacteria.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Split the five into two groups — this halves the work

---

### Group A — INHALED (3): Histoplasmosis · Blastomycosis · Coccidiomycosis

>> **All three follow the identical pattern:**

**Inhale spores → primary infection in the LUNGS → haematogenous spread → skin lesions**

---

### Group B — IMPLANTED (2): Sporotrichosis · Mycetoma

>> **Both follow the identical pattern:**

**Something sharp pushes the organism into the skin → local nodule → slow spread**

---

>> Learn **two patterns**, then learn only what distinguishes each disease. Far easier than five separate descriptions.

---

## Step 2: The inhaled three — what makes each one different

| | Organism | Spreads to | Distinctive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Histoplasmosis** | *H. capsulatum*, *H. duboisii* | Liver, bone marrow, spleen, lymph nodes, subcutaneous tissue | **Bat and poultry droppings**; African form umbilicates |
| **Blastomycosis** | *B. dermatitidis* | Skin, **bone, genital tract** | **Budding cells** on KOH |
| **Coccidiomycosis** | *C. immitis* | Skin, subcutaneous tissue, **bones** | **Panniculitis, abscesses, draining sinuses** |

---

## Step 3: Histoplasmosis — the two species differ, and it matters here

**Where does it come from?**

>> **Soil and the droppings of bats and poultry.**

So exposure is: caves, old buildings with bat roosts, chicken coops, and disturbing contaminated soil.

---

**_H. capsulatum_** → characteristically affects the **lungs**; **productive cough and constitutional symptoms**

---

**_H. duboisii_ — African histoplasmosis** → and this one is directly relevant to us

>> Presents as **subcutaneous nodules which tend to UMBILICATE and suppurate or ulcerate** as they grow.

**Umbilicate** = develop a central dimple, like a navel.

---

>> **"Umbilicated subcutaneous nodules in an African patient" should make you think _H. duboisii_.**

**Diagnosis:** culture from **sputum, bone marrow, lymph node biopsy, or pus/scrapings from ulcers**.

**Treatment:** IV **amphotericin B**; oral **itraconazole or ketoconazole**.

---

## Step 4: Blastomycosis — the shortest to remember

**Lungs → skin, bone and genital tract.**

**Skin:** irregularly crusted plaques, superficial ulcerative lesions, or firm subcutaneous nodules on **exposed** parts.

**Diagnosis:** **characteristic budding cells** on KOH.

**Treatment:** **IV amphotericin B** only.

>> Its one memorable feature is the **budding cells**. Attach the diagnosis to that.

---

## Step 5: Coccidiomycosis

**Lungs → skin, subcutaneous tissue and bones.**

**Skin:** **panniculitis** (inflammation of subcutaneous fat), **abscesses**, and **draining sinuses**.

>> A **draining sinus** is a tract from deep tissue to the skin surface, discharging continuously. It tells you the infection is **deep**, not superficial.

**Treatment:** oral **ketoconazole or itraconazole** — note, **not** amphotericin.

---

## Step 6: Sporotrichosis — the thorn disease

>> ***Sporothrix schenckii* grows on THORNY PLANTS.**

So the history is a **prick from a thorn, rose bush, or contact with plant material, hay, straw or soil**. Gardeners and farm workers.

---

**The sequence — learn it as a story:**

**1.** Firm, **painless or mildly tender subcutaneous nodule** at the site of trauma

**2.** Colour: **purple, violaceous, hyperpigmented brown, or dark grey**

**3.** The nodule **grows larger and becomes pustular**

**4.** It opens into a **chronic, hollowed-out sore (ulceration)**

---

>> **The distinctive feature: it starts PAINLESS.** A painless nodule after a thorn prick, which slowly enlarges and ulcerates.

**Spread:** by **lymphatic or haematogenous** routes to muscles, bones, joints. **Inhaled spores may cause lung infection.**

**Diagnosis:** microscopic demonstration in scrapings or biopsy.

**Treatment:** **potassium iodide**, IV amphotericin B, oral itraconazole.

>> **Potassium iodide is unique to sporotrichosis** in this lecture. An easy, high-yield association.

---

## Step 7: Mycetoma (Madura foot) — the one that is not always a fungus

**Chronic infection of the skin, subcutaneous tissue and BONES of the FEET.**

---

**Key facts:**

- Takes a **very long time** to develop
- **Rare in children** — because it takes years
- Introduced by **minor injuries — a splinter or thorn prick**
- Common in **agricultural workers**, who walk barefoot on contaminated soil

---

**And the important distinction:**

>> It can be caused by **either fungi OR bacteria**.

| Name | Cause |
|---|---|
| **EU**mycetoma | **Fungi** — *Pseudallescheria boydii*, *Madurella grisea*, *M. mycetomatis* |
| **ACTINO**mycetoma | **Filamentous bacteria** — *Nocardia brasiliensis*, *Actinomadura madurae*, *Streptomyces somaliensis* |

---

>> **Memory hook: EU = fungUs. ACTINO = actinomycetes = bacteria.**

**Why does the distinction matter?** Because an antifungal will not treat a bacterial actinomycetoma. **You must know which one you are treating.**

**Treatment given:** **itraconazole**.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Three inhaled → start in the LUNGS → spread by blood.**

>> **Two implanted → start where the thorn went in.**

>> **Thorn + painless nodule = sporotrichosis (potassium iodide).**

>> **Thorn + foot + years + bone = mycetoma (eu = fungus, actino = bacteria).**
cards.tie:
The deep mycoses look intimidating as a list of five.

As **two patterns with distinguishing details**, they become manageable.

And always ask the question that separates them: **inhaled, or implanted?**

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 1 — An 8-year-old boy is brought with an itchy, scaly patch on the scalp for 6 weeks, with hair loss. The hairs in the patch are broken at different lengths, and there are several patches giving a moth-eaten look. Two classmates have similar lesions and the family has a cat. What is your diagnosis and management?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis:** Tinea capitis.

**Basis:** A dermatophyte infection of the scalp, most often caused by *Trichophyton tonsurans*, occasionally by *Microsporum canis*. Lesions occur as erythematous and scaly circular plaques (ringworm) within which the infected hairs become **brittle and broken with different lengths**; multiple affected sites produce a **moth-eaten appearance**. Patients may complain of severe pruritus, and confluent patches of alopecia develop. The affected classmates reflect spread through close contact in institutions such as schools, while the cat raises *Microsporum canis*, which is acquired from pets.

**Investigations:** **scraping and KOH examination**. Culture of scrapings may be used to identify the species.

**Management:** **oral griseofulvin, itraconazole or terbinafine** — oral therapy is essential. Add a **topical antifungal cream or shampoo**. Examine and treat affected classmates and household contacts, and have the cat treated by a veterinarian, since *M. canis* is acquired from pets. Advise against sharing combs, caps, brushes, pillows and towels. Explain that hair will regrow once treated, as ordinary tinea capitis is non-scarring — unlike favus.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The broken hairs make the diagnosis

>> **"Broken at different lengths"** — this is the phrase that seals it.

---

**Why does it happen?**

**Step 1:** Dermatophytes eat **keratin**

**Step 2:** Hair is made of keratin

**Step 3:** The fungus grows **inside the hair shaft**

**Step 4:** The shaft becomes brittle and **snaps** — at whatever length it happens to weaken

---

>> The hairs are **broken**, not shed. That is completely different from alopecia areata, where hairs fall out cleanly from the root.

And multiple patches of unevenly broken hair give the **moth-eaten appearance**.

---

## Step 2: The classmates tell you it is transmissible

>> The lecturer lists **schools** among the institutions where closeness favours spread.

Children share combs, caps, brushes, pillows, and headrests.

---

>> **Two affected classmates is not incidental information.** It confirms the diagnosis *and* tells you the treatment must extend beyond this boy.

Treat him alone and he will be re-infected next term.

---

## Step 3: The cat is a specific, deliberate clue

>> Tinea is *"usually acquired from direct contact from infected person, surfaces and **pets** (*M. canis*)"*.

---

**_Microsporum **canis**_ — canis means dog**, but it infects **cats** too, and cats are the commoner source.

---

>> **The cat must be examined and treated by a vet**, or it remains a reservoir re-infecting the household indefinitely.

This is the step students almost always omit.

---

## Step 4: Why is oral treatment ESSENTIAL, not optional?

This is the single most important management point.

---

**Where is the fungus?**

>> **Inside the hair shaft.**

**Can a cream get inside a hair shaft?**

>> **No.** It sits on the scalp surface.

---

**So how does an oral drug reach it?**

Griseofulvin, itraconazole and terbinafine are carried in the **blood to the hair follicle**, and are **incorporated into the growing hair** — reaching the fungus from the inside.

---

>> **Topical treatment alone will always fail in tinea capitis.** This is why the lecturer lists oral drugs first.

---

## Step 5: Then why bother with the shampoo at all?

Good question, and it has a good answer.

---

>> The shampoo does not cure the child. It **reduces spores on the scalp surface** — so he is **less infectious to other children**.

**Oral = cure the patient. Topical = protect the class.**

---

## Step 6: What must you look for that would change everything?

Two complications to exclude on examination:

---

**KERION** — a boggy, inflamed mass with crusts and pustules

>> Caused by the child's **own cellular immune response**, not by bacteria.

**Do not incise it. Do not treat it as an abscess.** It needs antifungals.

---

**FAVUS** — thick yellow **cup-shaped scutula** around follicles, with a **mousy odour**

>> Caused by *Trichophyton schoenleinii*, and it causes **permanent scarring alopecia**.

This boy has neither — his is ordinary tinea capitis.

---

## Step 7: What do you tell the parents about the hair?

This matters enormously to a family.

---

>> **Ordinary tinea capitis is NON-scarring.** The follicles are intact, so **hair regrows** after treatment.

>> **Favus IS scarring.** The follicles are destroyed, and hair **never** regrows.

---

So you can reassure this family — provided you have looked carefully and excluded favus.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Broken hairs at different lengths + moth-eaten patches = tinea capitis.**

>> **Treat ORALLY. Treat the classmates. Treat the cat. Stop the sharing.**

>> **And reassure them the hair will come back.**
cards.tie:
This case shows why the introduction talks about **schools, crowding and shared items**.

The epidemiology is not background — it is half of the management plan.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 2 — A 14-year-old girl complains of patches on her chest, neck and back that are lighter than her surrounding skin, present for 5 months. They are covered in a fine scale and have merged in places. They are not itchy or painful. She sweats heavily and wears tight clothing. She is otherwise well. What is your diagnosis and management?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis:** Pityriasis versicolor.

**Basis:** A common **chronic** fungal infection of the **stratum corneum**, caused by the dimorphic yeast ***Malassezia furfur***, found in areas of skin **rich in sebum production**. It is most prevalent in **adolescents and young adults**. The lesions are **hypopigmented or hyperpigmented macules covered with a fine scale that often merge to form confluent patches**, occurring most commonly on the **neck, upper chest, back and upper arms**. Her predisposing factors are a **warm humid environment, excessive sweating and occlusion** from tight clothing.

**Investigations:** **Wood lamp** examination; **KOH preparation of scrapings**; skin biopsy if the diagnosis remains in doubt.

**Management:** **selenium sulfide suspension applied overnight**; or **imidazole or terbinafine cream twice daily for 2–4 weeks**. Oral therapy with **ketoconazole, fluconazole or itraconazole** for extensive or resistant disease. Advise loose, non-occlusive cotton clothing, and keeping the skin dry. **Explain that the pale patches will take months to repigment after the fungus has been cleared**, and that recurrence is common because *Malassezia* is part of normal skin flora.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The distribution is the diagnosis

**Chest, neck and back** in a **14-year-old**.

---

**Why exactly those areas?**

>> ***Malassezia furfur* is lipophilic** — it feeds on the fats in **sebum**.

So it grows where the **sebaceous glands are densest**: neck, upper chest, back and upper arms.

---

>> **The distribution is a map of sebaceous gland density.** Nothing random about it.

---

## Step 2: Why a 14-year-old?

Follow the hormones.

---

**Step 1:** Puberty → androgen surge

**Step 2:** Androgens **stimulate sebaceous glands**

**Step 3:** Sebum production rises sharply

**Step 4:** More food for a lipophilic yeast

---

>> Which is exactly why the lecturer says it is **most prevalent in adolescents and young adults** — and why acne appears at the same age in the same places.

**Follow the sebum.**

---

## Step 3: Her two predisposing factors are in the history

>> **"She sweats heavily"** → *excessive sweating* — a listed predisposing factor

>> **"Wears tight clothing"** → *occlusion* — also a listed predisposing factor

Plus our **warm humid environment** — the third.

---

>> Three of the seven predisposing factors, present in one sentence. The history is doing the diagnostic work.

---

## Step 4: Why is it not itchy?

This is a useful negative.

---

>> The infection is confined to the **stratum corneum** — the **dead**, outermost layer.

It does not reach living tissue, so there is little inflammation and little symptom.

---

**Compare with tinea corporis**, which is itchy and inflamed with an active vesicular edge.

>> **A non-itchy, scaly, discoloured patch is much more likely to be versicolor.**

---

## Step 5: Why are the patches LIGHTER than her skin?

Understand this properly — it also explains what happens after treatment.

---

**Step 1:** *Malassezia* produces **dicarboxylic acids**

**Step 2:** These **inhibit tyrosinase**, the key enzyme in melanin production

**Step 3:** Less melanin is made in that patch

**Step 4:** The patch appears **hypopigmented** — lighter than surrounding skin

---

>> And in other people, or other patches, it appears **hyper**pigmented instead. That is why it is called **versi**color — *varying* colour.

---

## Step 6: The Wood lamp — quick and satisfying

Ultraviolet light.

>> Pityriasis versicolor **fluoresces**, typically golden-yellow.

---

**Two benefits:**

- Confirms the diagnosis instantly, with no laboratory
- Shows the **full extent** — including patches you cannot see in ordinary light

**KOH** shows the classic mixture of short hyphae and round spores.

---

## Step 7: Why selenium sulfide, and why overnight?

---

**Why this drug?**

Her disease covers **chest, neck and back** — a large surface area.

>> A **suspension** covers wide areas cheaply and evenly. Creams are impractical and expensive over that much skin.

---

**Why overnight?**

>> It needs **prolonged contact time** with the stratum corneum to work.

---

**And note the duration of the cream option:** imidazole or terbinafine **twice daily for 2–4 WEEKS**.

>> This is a **chronic** infection. A few days of treatment will not clear it, and patients who stop early relapse.

---

## Step 8: The conversation that prevents her coming back angry

This is the most important part of the consultation.

---

>> **Tell her the pale patches will NOT disappear when the fungus does.**

The yeast is killed within weeks. But melanin production has been suppressed, and it takes **months** for pigment to return.

---

**If you do not explain this:**

- She thinks the treatment failed
- She keeps applying creams unnecessarily
- Or she loses faith and stops attending

---

**Also warn her about recurrence:**

>> ***Malassezia* is part of normal skin flora.** You are reducing an **overgrowth**, not eradicating an invader.

And her predisposing factors — heat, humidity, sweating, sebum — are all still present.

---

## Step 9: The advice that is genuinely treatment

- **Loose cotton clothing** instead of tight — removes the **occlusion**
- **Keep the skin dry** — removes the moisture

>> These attack the predisposing factors directly, and they reduce recurrence more than any drug does.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Adolescent + sebum-rich sites + fine scaly patches that vary in colour + not itchy = pityriasis versicolor.**

>> **Wood lamp confirms. Selenium sulfide overnight treats. And warn her the colour takes months to return.**
cards.tie:
This case rewards understanding the **mechanism of the pigment change**.

Without it, you cannot have the one conversation that determines whether she trusts the treatment.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 3 — A 10-year-old boy who helps on his family's farm presents with a firm, painless dark nodule on his right forearm which appeared 3 weeks after a thorn prick. It has enlarged, become pustular, and has now opened into a hollowed-out ulcer. There are similar smaller nodules tracking up his arm. What is your diagnosis and management?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis:** Sporotrichosis.

**Basis:** A chronic infection which follows **accidental implantation into the skin of _Sporothrix schenckii_, which grows on thorny plants**. It typically begins as a **firm, painless or mildly tender subcutaneous nodule at the site of skin trauma**, presenting as a **purple, violaceous, hyperpigmented brown or dark grey nodule**. The primary nodule **grows larger, becomes pustular, and may open into a chronic, hollowed-out sore (ulceration)**. Early lesions are indurated subcutaneous nodules at the sites of implantation. The nodules tracking up the arm reflect **spread by the lymphatic route**; infection may also spread haematogenously to muscles, bones, joints or other tissues.

**Investigations:** **microscopic demonstration of the fungus in preparations from scrapings or biopsy**.

**Management:** **potassium iodide**; or oral **itraconazole**; **IV amphotericin B** for severe or disseminated disease. Advise protective clothing and gloves for farm work, and prompt cleaning of thorn pricks and minor injuries. Warn that **inhalation of spores may cause lung infection**, and assess for systemic spread if he becomes unwell.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The history hands you the diagnosis

>> **Thorn prick** + **farm work** + a nodule appearing weeks later.

---

Go back to the lecture:

>> ***Sporothrix schenckii* grows on THORNY PLANTS.**

And the infection follows **accidental implantation into the skin**.

---

>> **This is the only deep mycosis in the lecture caught from a thorn.**

If a question mentions a thorn, a rose bush, hay, straw or plant material, think sporotrichosis.

---

## Step 2: Note what makes it different from the other deep mycoses

**Histoplasmosis, blastomycosis and coccidiomycosis are all INHALED.**

They start in the **lungs** and spread by blood.

---

>> **Sporotrichosis is IMPLANTED.** It starts exactly where the thorn went in.

That is why the nodule is on the forearm — the site of the injury — and not in his chest.

---

## Step 3: "Painless" is a deliberate and important clue

>> The lecturer specifies: **firm, PAINLESS or mildly tender** subcutaneous nodule.

---

**Why does that matter?**

A bacterial abscess after a thorn prick would be **hot, red and very painful** within days.

---

>> **Painless + slow + weeks later = fungal, not bacterial.**

The delay matters too: **3 weeks** after the injury. Bacterial infection appears in days.

---

## Step 4: The colour is characteristic

>> **Purple, violaceous, hyperpigmented brown, or dark grey.**

Not the bright red of acute bacterial inflammation.

---

>> A **dark, dusky** nodule suggests a chronic, indolent process — again pointing away from bacteria.

---

## Step 5: The sequence has followed the lecture exactly

Check this boy against the described course:

---

**1. Firm painless nodule at the trauma site** ✓

**2. Grows larger** ✓

**3. Becomes pustular** ✓

**4. Opens into a chronic hollowed-out sore (ulceration)** ✓

---

>> Every stage matches. The lecturer's description is not a list — it is a **timeline**, and this boy has walked through it.

---

## Step 6: What do the nodules tracking up his arm mean?

This is the finding that confirms everything.

---

>> The lecturer: *"Infection may spread by **lymphatic** or haematogenous routes."*

---

**What is happening:**

**Step 1:** The fungus enters the local **lymphatic vessels**

**Step 2:** Lymph flows **from the hand towards the trunk**

**Step 3:** Fungus is deposited along the way

**Step 4:** New nodules form **in a line following the lymphatic channel**

---

>> A **chain of nodules tracking proximally up a limb from a puncture wound** is close to diagnostic of sporotrichosis.

The pattern is telling you the route of spread.

---

## Step 7: How is it confirmed?

>> **Microscopic demonstration of the fungus in preparations from scrapings or biopsy.**

---

Biopsy is more reliable here than a surface scraping, because the organism is **subcutaneous** — under the skin, not on it.

>> This contrasts with the superficial mycoses, where a simple KOH scraping suffices. **Deep infection needs deep sampling.**

---

## Step 8: Why potassium iodide — the odd one out in the whole lecture?

>> **Potassium iodide is unique to sporotrichosis** among everything in this topic.

---

It is an old, cheap treatment, given orally, and it works well in the cutaneous and lymphocutaneous forms — which is exactly what this boy has.

---

**The alternatives:**

- **Oral itraconazole** — a modern azole, well tolerated
- **IV amphotericin B** — reserved for **severe or disseminated** disease

---

>> **Match the drug to the extent of disease.** Localised and lymphocutaneous → potassium iodide or itraconazole. Disseminated → amphotericin B.

---

## Step 9: What else must you think about?

>> The lecturer warns: *"**Inhalation of spores may cause lung infection.**"*

---

So if he develops cough or constitutional symptoms, think pulmonary sporotrichosis.

And spread can reach **muscles, bones and joints** — so examine for joint swelling or bone tenderness.

---

## Step 10: Prevention — because he will go back to the farm

- **Protective clothing and gloves** for farm work
- **Prompt cleaning** of thorn pricks and minor injuries

>> He is going to keep working on the farm. Treatment without prevention just delays the next episode.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Thorn prick + painless dark nodule weeks later + ulcerates + nodules tracking up the limb = sporotrichosis.**

>> **Biopsy to confirm. Potassium iodide or itraconazole to treat. Gloves to prevent.**
cards.tie:
This case is the clearest example of the **inhaled versus implanted** split.

Ask that one question of any deep mycosis, and the whole differential narrows immediately.

cards.q: KEY CUES — What words and findings point to each fungal skin infection?
cards.lecturer: Summary of the discriminating features drawn across the whole lecture.
cards.explain:
## First — the two questions that sort everything

>> **1. Superficial or deep?** (skin/hair/nails/mucosa, or lungs/bone/viscera)

>> **2. If deep — inhaled or implanted?**

---

## CANDIDIASIS cues

**"Curdy, cheesy, whitish-grey plaques on reddened mucosa"**

→ **Oral thrush.**

---

**"Scraping the plaque causes punctate bleeding"**

→ **Confirms thrush** — the plaques invade the mucosa superficially.

>> Milk curds wipe off cleanly with **no** bleeding.

---

**"Dusky red macerated lesions in skin folds"** — neck, flanks, large flexures, limbs

→ **Cutaneous candidiasis.**

---

**"Prolonged antibiotics" · "steroids" · "diabetes" · "HIV" · "leukaemia"**

→ Predisposing to candidiasis. **Always ask WHY this patient has thrush.**

---

## TINEA CAPITIS cues

**"Hairs broken at different lengths" · "moth-eaten appearance"**

→ **TINEA CAPITIS.** *Trichophyton tonsurans* most often.

>> **Always treat ORALLY** — the fungus is inside the hair shaft.

---

**"Boggy inflamed mass with crusts and pustules"**

→ **KERION** — caused by the host's own **cellular immune response**.

>> **Do NOT incise. It is not a bacterial abscess.**

---

**"Yellow cup-shaped crusts (scutula)" + "mousy odour" + "honey-combed"**

→ **FAVUS** — *Trichophyton schoenleinii*.

>> Causes **PERMANENT SCARRING alopecia**. Ordinary tinea capitis does not.

---

## TINEA CORPORIS cues

**"Annular / ring lesion, clear centre, active scaly edge with vesicles"**

→ **TINEA CORPORIS.**

>> **Scrape the EDGE, not the centre** — that is where the living fungus is.

---

**"Pet cat or dog"**

→ ***Microsporum canis***. **Treat the pet** or it recurs.

---

**Naming by site:** faciale = face · axillaris = axilla · cruris = groin · manuum = hands · **unguium = nails**

---

## TINEA PEDIS cues

**"Fissured, macerated, peeling skin between the toes" + "foul odour" + "itchy"**

→ **TINEA PEDIS** (athlete's foot). *T. rubrum*, *T. mentagrophytes*, ***Epidermophyton floccosum***.

---

>> **THE LINK:** those fissures are a **classic entry point for CELLULITIS**. Treating the fungus prevents the bacterial infection.

**Treat:** avoid occlusive footwear · **dry between the toes** · zinc undecylenate powder · imidazole cream.

---

## PITYRIASIS VERSICOLOR cues

**"Patches lighter OR darker than surrounding skin" + "fine scale" + "merging"**

→ **PITYRIASIS VERSICOLOR** — *Malassezia furfur*.

---

**"Neck, upper chest, back, upper arms" + "adolescent" + "not itchy"**

→ Same. **Follow the sebum** — the yeast is lipophilic.

---

**"Fluoresces under Wood lamp"** → confirms it.

**Treat:** **selenium sulfide overnight** · imidazole/terbinafine cream **2–4 weeks**.

>> **Warn: pale patches take MONTHS to repigment after cure.**

---

## DEEP MYCOSES — the inhaled three

> All: **inhaled → LUNGS → haematogenous spread → skin**

---

**"Bat or poultry droppings" · "caves" · "soil"**

→ **HISTOPLASMOSIS.**

---

**"Umbilicated subcutaneous nodules that suppurate or ulcerate"**

→ ***H. duboisii*** — **African histoplasmosis**.

---

**"Budding cells on KOH" + "bone and genital tract"**

→ **BLASTOMYCOSIS.** Treat: **IV amphotericin B**.

---

**"Panniculitis, abscesses and draining sinuses"**

→ **COCCIDIOMYCOSIS.** Treat: **oral ketoconazole or itraconazole**.

---

## DEEP MYCOSES — the implanted two

**"Thorn prick" + "painless dark nodule" + "ulcerates" + "nodules tracking up the limb"**

→ **SPOROTRICHOSIS.**

>> Treat with **POTASSIUM IODIDE** — unique to this condition in the lecture.

---

**"Splinter or thorn" + "FOOT" + "bone involvement" + "years to develop" + "agricultural worker"**

→ **MYCETOMA (Madura foot).** **Rare in children.**

>> **EU**mycetoma = **fungus** · **ACTINO**mycetoma = **filamentous bacteria**

---

## Diagnostic tool cues

| Cue | Tool |
|---|---|
| Any superficial tinea | **Scraping + KOH** |
| Pityriasis versicolor | **Wood lamp** |
| Species identification | **Culture** |
| Deep mycosis | **Biopsy, sputum, bone marrow, lymph node** |

---

## Treatment traps

>> **Tinea capitis treated with cream alone** → **WRONG.** Needs oral.

>> **Kerion incised as an abscess** → **WRONG.** It is fungal + immune.

>> **Athlete's foot ignored in a patient with recurrent leg cellulitis** → **WRONG.** That is the entry point.

>> **Versicolor patient told treatment failed because patches remain** → **WRONG.** Repigmentation takes months.
cards.tie:
This is your fast-recall layer.

And note how often the answer is not a drug:

**dry the toe webs · treat the cat · loosen the clothing · treat the classmates.**
